text: The ball is to the left of the cup, the cup is behind the book, and the book is on the table. Where is the ball relative to the book?
The first thing you taste is salt, then a bright pop of lime, then the slow burn of chili creeping across your tongue, clinging to the roof of your mouth long after you swallow.
Head east on Main Street past the red water tower. Turn left at the old oak tree that's split by lightning. My house is the third one on the right, with a rusted bicycle in the front yard.

Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Slowly lift your hips toward the ceiling, squeezing your glutes at the top, then lower vertebra by vertebra until your tailbone touches the mat.

He pivoted on his left foot, dropping his center of gravity as the fist grazed his ear. He drove his right shoulder into the attacker's sternum, feeling the ribs give slightly, and hooked his ankle behind the other man's knee to sweep him off balance.

It starts as a faint pressure behind my left eye, then builds into a hammering throb that syncs with my heartbeat. Light feels like needles, sound is a physical weight pressing down, and my own skin feels too tight, as if my skull is slowly being split open from the inside.
The coffee shop smelled like burnt sugar and wet wool. The barista's hands moved in a blur—tamping, steaming, pouring—while the hiss of the espresso machine punctuated every order like a heartbeat.
A worm of unease crawled from my stomach up into my throat, cold and slick. My palms were damp, and the room seemed to tilt just slightly, as if the floor had turned to water under my feet.

You are in a vast, circular hall. The only light comes from a narrow slit high in the dome. Carved into the floor is a spiral that narrows toward the center, where a single black stone sits. There is no other exit.
The man was eight feet away, then five, then two. Each step was a deliberate compression of space. I could smell the sweat on his jacket. When he stopped, his shadow covered my feet.

The butter sizzled and popped as it slid across the hot pan, releasing a nutty, almost toasted aroma that filled the kitchen. I scraped the spatula along the bottom, feeling the thin, golden crust forming on the fish fillet.

Walk through the main entrance: a low, dark marble vestibule that smells of cold stone and damp earth. Turn left, and the corridor opens into a double-height atrium flooded with west-facing light, where the polished concrete floor reflects the oak ceiling above.

This Châteauneuf-du-Pape has a deep garnet core. On the nose, crushed blackberries, dried Provencal herbs, and a whisper of white pepper. The palate is full-bodied, with grippy tannins that coat the cheeks like fine sand, and a long, warm finish that lingers with hints of garrigue and licorice.

The left foot is on a small, sloping crimp at knee height; drop the right knee outward to smear the right foot on the vertical panel. Reach up with the left hand to a pinch—it’s positive but slightly greasy—then bump the right hand to a small undercling, pulling hard to keep the hips in.

First time I stepped in the snow, it was so quiet. The cold bit my nose right away, and my boots made a crunch-crunch sound. I fell backward, and it felt soft and cold on my cheeks, like a giant, freezing blanket.

The user stands at the center of a circular stone platform, ten meters in diameter, under a violet sky with twin moons. A low-poly forest of glowing blue ferns surrounds the platform; every fifth fern pulses slowly. The user can walk or teleport up to 20 meters. A river of liquid mercury flows clockwise at the forest edge, its surface reflecting the moons with a metallic sheen.

I hear the bus stop’s beeper, about 20 steps ahead and slightly to the left. The ground under my cane changes from rough concrete to smooth tile—that means the covered walkway. A warm draft hits my left side, so the bakery door must be open again. The smell of bread tells me I'm exactly at the entrance.
The ball is left of the cup. The cup is behind the book. The book is on the table. Is the ball to the left or the right of the table?

A sharp, stabbing pain runs from my lower back down the outside of my right leg, like a hot wire being pulled from my spine to my big toe. When I stand up, the left knee buckles slightly, and I feel a grinding sensation deep in the joint, as if gravel is trapped between the bones.

The point guard fakes left, then crosses over hard to his right, planting his left foot at the free-throw line. He elevates—his shoulders square to the rim—and releases the ball with a high arc. The defender’s hand brushes his wrist a tenth of a second late, and the crowd exhales as the ball rotates cleanly through the net.
